neighborhoods.name: Morgon
neighborhoods.description: The schist and iron-rich cru centred on the village of Villié-Morgon and the Cote du Py hill. Morgon Gamay deepens from purple cherry toward earthy, savoury complexity with age, and the appellation is the spiritual home of the Gang of Four natural-wine movement.
neighborhoods.vibe: Schist hillsides and the Cote du Py volcanic rise, where natural winemakers transformed a humble cru into a global touchstone for low-intervention Gamay.

neighborhoods.name: Cote du Py
neighborhoods.description: The volcanic blue-schist and manganese hill within Morgon, a noted single lieu-dit in Beaujolais. Cote du Py Gamay is denser, darker and more mineral than village Morgon, with a graphite and iron edge that develops over years. Jean Foillard's Cote du Py is the principal expression of the site.
neighborhoods.vibe: A single ridge of blue volcanic schist and iron-rich soils where Gamay takes on a power and mineral weight unlike anywhere else in Beaujolais.

neighborhoods.name: Fleurie
neighborhoods.description: The cru known as the queen of Beaujolais for its perfumed, floral character. Pink and grey granite soils produce Gamay with rose petal, violet and peach aromas and a silky texture.
neighborhoods.vibe: A hilltop village crowned with a small chapel, surrounded by granite slopes that produce the most perfumed and delicate Gamay in Beaujolais.

neighborhoods.name: Moulin-a-Vent
neighborhoods.description: The most powerful and age-worthy of the ten crus, built on granite and manganese-rich soils around the iconic 15th-century windmill lieu-dit near Romanèche-Thorins. With time in bottle, Moulin-a-Vent Gamay develops the brooding, mineral complexity associated with Pinot Noir.
neighborhoods.vibe: Granite slopes threaded with manganese and centred on a solitary 15th-century windmill, producing the most structured and cellar-worthy Gamay in the region.

neighborhoods.name: Saint-Amour
neighborhoods.description: The northernmost of the ten crus, straddling the border with the Maconnais in Saone-et-Loire. Sandy granite soils produce fragrant, cherry-bright Gamay with a light-medium body, beloved for its evocative name and approachable style.
neighborhoods.vibe: The most northerly cru, where Saone-et-Loire sandy granite produces the lightest and most aromatic Gamay with a name that makes it a Valentine's Day favourite.

neighborhoods.name: Julienas
neighborhoods.description: One of the four original Beaujolais crus (alongside Chenas, Moulin-a-Vent and Fleurie) with vineyards spread across granite and schist soils in Saone-et-Loire and Rhone. Julienas produces spicy, full-bodied Gamay with good structure and a medium ageing window.
neighborhoods.vibe: A handsome hillside village and one of the four original crus, producing spiced and structured Gamay from granite and schist on the Saone-et-Loire border.

neighborhoods.name: Chenas
neighborhoods.description: The smallest of the ten crus in total area, sandwiched between Moulin-a-Vent and Julienas. Granite and sandy soils produce powerful, spiced Gamay that overlaps stylistically with its neighbour Moulin-a-Vent. Many Chenas vineyards historically sold their fruit under the Moulin-a-Vent label.
neighborhoods.vibe: The smallest and least-known cru, squeezed between Julienas and Moulin-a-Vent, producing powerful, spicy Gamay from granite and sand that rewards patient discovery.

neighborhoods.name: Chiroubles
neighborhoods.description: The highest-altitude cru in Beaujolais at 400 metres and above, producing the lightest and most fragrant Gamay of all ten. Cool granitic slopes yield wines of great delicacy, high natural acidity and early-drinking charm. The village belvedere overlooks the entire Beaujolais vineyard.
neighborhoods.vibe: Beaujolais' highest cru, a cool granite plateau above 400 metres where Gamay turns feather-light, violet-scented and wonderfully refreshing.

neighborhoods.name: Régnié
neighborhoods.description: The youngest of the ten crus, granted AOC status in 1988. Granitic and sandy soils around Régnié-Durette produce fresh, fruit-forward Gamay with a light-medium body and an accessible, early-drinking character. Christian Ducroux is the appellation's foremost Demeter-certified biodynamic producer.
neighborhoods.vibe: Beaujolais' newest cru as of 1988, an approachable granitic zone producing fresh, cherry-fruited Gamay and home to the region's leading biodynamic producers.

neighborhoods.name: Brouilly
neighborhoods.description: The largest of the ten crus in area, spread across six communes including Saint-Lager, Cercié, Odenas and Saint-Etienne-la-Varenne around the dramatic Mont Brouilly dome. Granite and schist soils produce generous, round Gamay with good fruit and approachable structure.
neighborhoods.vibe: The largest and most varied cru, circling the extinct volcano of Mont Brouilly across six communes, producing ripe, generous Gamay in a broad, accessible style.

neighborhoods.name: Cote de Brouilly
neighborhoods.description: The small cru occupying the steep volcanic basalt slopes of Mont Brouilly itself, sitting inside the larger Brouilly appellation. Blue volcanic rock and poor basalt soils produce more concentrated, mineral and structured Gamay than village Brouilly, with a distinctive stony savouriness.
neighborhoods.vibe: The volcanic crown of Mont Brouilly, where blue basalt soils create the most mineral and concentrated Gamay in the Brouilly family, and the pisse-vieille legend lives on.

neighborhoods.name: Beaujolais-Villages
neighborhoods.description: The middle tier of the Beaujolais appellation pyramid, covering 38 named communes in the granite and schist northern belt. A step above basic Beaujolais in body and structure, Beaujolais-Villages can often approach cru character at a fraction of the price. Includes both still and Nouveau variants.
neighborhoods.vibe: The middle tier spanning 38 granite communes between the flat Beaujolais plain and the named crus, delivering reliable, honest Gamay at accessible prices.
